video_title: From Skid Row Showers to Short Selling Master · David Capablanca
gist: Short seller David Capablanca details how he built a small-cap short-selling edge shorting COVID-era premarket biotech fades and meme-stock manias, then walks through the mechanics of small-cap pump-and-dumps, predatory Chinese short-squeeze algorithms, and the survival rules (float, market cap, catalyst filters) he uses to avoid career-ending losses.

insights.claim: Capablanca's 2020 breakout came from a single premarket pattern: shorting COVID-era biotech stocks that spiked overnight then faded back toward the market open, traded in a 4am-7am ET window.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Illustrates that a durable early edge can rest on one repeatable intraday/premarket fade pattern during mania regimes rather than a broad strategy.
insights.quote: I would wake up and look at my screen in front of me. And I was like, Wow, all these stocks were fading down.

insights.claim: Capablanca turned the premarket biotech-fade pattern into $150,000 in profits in 2020, then made roughly another $100,000 shorting the same premarket setup during the meme-stock mania of January-February 2021.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Shows the size of edge available in mania premarket tape and that the same pattern recurred across successive 2020-2021 bubbles.

insights.claim: Capablanca says he stopped trading premarket entirely about two years ago because he no longer sees an edge there, having shifted mostly to intraday setups.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Signals that the premarket small-cap short edge has eroded; edges are regime-specific and decay, forcing strategy migration.
insights.quote: I stopped trading premarket for a long time. I haven't traded premarket in like two years at all. Zero.

insights.claim: Capablanca frames short sellers as reactive and long traders as predictive: shorts should short into strength and react off a confirmed backside rather than predict a top and short weakness.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: A reusable execution rule that separates high-probability shorts (react off the squeeze) from account-killing early shorts.
insights.quote: Short on strength never chase weakness.

insights.claim: Capablanca describes his preferred short as 'mop-up duty' on a midday consolidation that holds its highs into the power hour, shorting only after the anticipated short squeeze actually fires.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Concrete pattern: the same setup longs buy predictively for a power-hour squeeze is one shorts fade reactively after it pops.
insights.quote: I call like mop-up duty. I'm doing mop-up duty from the shorts that get squeezed.

insights.claim: Capablanca refuses to trade Chinese-listed small caps entirely, arguing they are deliberately engineered to squeeze short sellers and can inflict career-ending losses.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Cutting an entire category (China, plus Singapore) is presented as a survival filter that removes the worst tail-risk squeezes.
insights.quote: the Chinese stocks, they are they get hijacked and they have these crazy squeezes and they can cost you a career.

insights.claim: Capablanca claims manipulators of Chinese pumps first dumped shares on gullible longs via WhatsApp groups (2021-22), then reversed strategy once short sellers piled in, specifically targeting shorts because shorts carry bigger accounts and more liquidity.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Explains why crowded short trades in China names became the prey: the manipulation adapts to where the money is.
insights.quote: they realized that short sellers have more money than long traders. So they started to pinpoint the short sellers and squeeze them.

insights.claim: Capablanca describes a reverse-engineered predatory squeeze algorithm that aggregates share locates across brokers, maps where shorts entered, and calculates the share volume needed to push price past each account's pain threshold before it taps out.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Frames modern squeezes as data-driven liquidation hunts; shorts should assume their entry and pain point are being modeled.
insights.quote: you develop an algorithm that aggregates all the locates from all the short selling brokers... then they got to calculate how many shares would it take to squeeze them

insights.claim: Capablanca estimates a typical $50k short account taps out around a $10,000-$15,000 loss, and says Chinese pumps deliberately push price to 2-3x that pain threshold versus American squeezes that stop where manipulators take profit.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Quantifies why Chinese squeezes are uniquely dangerous: they overshoot the normal capitulation point by design.
insights.quote: the Chinese ones do it more than usual. They will send it like two 3x that pain threshold.

insights.claim: Capablanca argues Chinese small-cap manipulation persists because ~90% of Chinese small caps have unaudited, likely fudged financials showing absurd cash balances, and underwriters pay exchanges to look the other way rather than delist.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Points to weak enforcement and favoritism as the structural reason the category stays exploitable.
insights.quote: most of their finances are unodudited... 90% of the Chinese stocks and small caps, they all have an absurd amount of cash.

insights.claim: Capablanca says the predatory-squeeze behavior is contained almost entirely to Chinese and Singapore-listed stocks, so excluding those two categories removes most of the risk.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Actionable universe filter: geography of listing is a primary manipulation screen.
insights.quote: the beauty is that it's contained to just Chinese stocks and also Singapore.

insights.claim: Capablanca references The China Hustle documentary and a hedge-fund investigator jailed in China for probing a company, arguing COVID (through ~2023) made Chinese firms uninvestigable and thus fertile ground for fraud.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Inaccessibility for due diligence is a red flag that enables manipulation; a due-diligence gap is itself tradable signal.

insights.claim: Capablanca reads after-hours patent news as a bearish pump catalyst, not innovation: releases typically hit ~5 minutes after the close, spike a stock 80-100%+, then fade, serving as a liquidity event to let insider bag-holders exit.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Gives a specific, recurring after-hours short setup with a behavioral rationale (illiquid tape used to sneak price higher for exits).
insights.quote: when a company puts out patent news, it's nothing innovative... It's a liquidity event for certain players inside to get out.

insights.claim: Capablanca invokes an Elon Musk view that patents merely block competitors rather than advance an industry, using it to argue patent announcements carry no real fundamental value for a stock.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Reframes a common 'good news' catalyst as noise, supporting fading patent-driven spikes.

insights.claim: Capablanca treats a company changing its ticker to chase a hot theme (e.g. adding 'AI') as an almost-always bearish tell, comparing it to 1999 dot-com boom firms appending '.com' and citing NFTG shifting from NFTs in 2021 to AI.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: A cheap screen: bandwagon rebrands flag promotional stocks likely to be pump-and-dumps.
insights.quote: Why are they changing their ticket? Oh, now it's AI. So they're trying to jump on the AI bandwagon.

insights.claim: Capablanca explains that a dilutive offering is only a good short when insiders actually dump the offering shares into the float (raising supply and selling pressure); if they don't dump, the stock just has a quick panic and can squeeze back.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Refines the popular 'short the offering' trade: the edge depends on actual supply expansion, not the headline price.
insights.quote: when they do an offering supply increases... that adds selling pressure downward... But if they don't dump that then it's just it just sells off because of quick panic.

insights.claim: Capablanca sniffs out pump-and-dumps by treating a >40% intraday move or compensated promotional text messages as the 'broken tail light' that triggers a deep dive into underwriters and insiders.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Concrete detection trigger and investigative workflow for identifying manipulated names to short.
insights.quote: a stock gets on my radar maybe it's going up too fast it hits 40% up... now it's like the broken tail light I'm looking

insights.claim: Capablanca ties promotional small-cap pumps to IPO lockup expirations: underwriters engineer new highs into and through the lockup so insiders can dump unlocked shares days after, after which the stock collapses.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Lockup-expiration calendars around freshly-pumped names flag high-probability short windows.
insights.quote: The agenda is the lockup expiration... after the lock up a few days later it just dumps

insights.claim: Capablanca alleges Chinese pumps use wash trading to fake demand on the bid and ask, aided by rules that don't apply overseas the way they do domestically.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Order-book demand in these names can be artificial, undermining tape-reading signals traders rely on.

insights.claim: Capablanca's core risk mantra is that no single trade is worth your career and that the goal is to survive your learning curve, because the millions come only to traders who avoid ruin.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: The overriding survival principle: position and stock selection should be optimized for longevity over any single opportunity.
insights.quote: survive your learning curve and never risk ruin. No one trade is worth your career.

insights.claim: Capablanca's exclusion checklist for shortable small caps: no sub-1.5 million float, no Chinese or Singapore stocks, no high institutional ownership, no high-debt stocks, and no market cap over ~$250 million.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: A concrete, replicable screen defining which small caps to avoid to sidestep the worst squeezes.
insights.quote: no sub 1.5 million floats, no Chinese stocks. I would throw the Singapore stocks in there... no high institutional ownership... no high debt stocks... no over 250 million market cap

insights.claim: Capablanca advises entering biotech shorts only above the dilution/offering price, since that level defines where insider supply enters and where the risk/reward flips.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: A specific entry rule that anchors biotech shorts to known dilution mechanics rather than arbitrary levels.
insights.quote: Biotech stocks, you got to enter over the dilution prices. This is an important one.

insights.claim: Capablanca says sub-1-million-float 'nano' stocks can be manipulated via an algorithm combining locate inventory, time-and-sales data, and the exact percentage move needed to trigger a halt so operators capture the gap.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Explains why ultra-low-float names are uniquely riggable around halt mechanics, justifying a hard float floor.

insights.claim: Capablanca argues it is better to hit rock bottom as a trader when you have little capital, because early large losses teach the rules while the dollar stakes are survivable.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Frames early drawdowns as cheap tuition; scaling size before internalizing rules is the real danger.

insights.claim: Capablanca cites HKD (AMTD Digital) running from ~$7 to ~$2,500 in 2022 as the first NYSE-listed Chinese stock to squeeze that violently, and notes no NYSE stock has repeated it since because the exchange tightened standards afterward.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Marks a historic squeeze magnitude and suggests NYSE listings are now relatively safer than NASDAQ for this tail risk.
insights.quote: HKD was the first New York Stock Exchange Chinese stock to do that. By the way, there hasn't been any New York Stock Exchange listed stock to do that since.

insights.claim: Capablanca reports that during HKD's all-time-high squeeze, NYSE market makers he met were simply laughing about it and doing nothing, teaching him that short sellers cannot depend on the exchange to save them.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Reinforces that manipulation goes unenforced in real time; traders must self-protect via stops and stock selection.
insights.quote: the market makers were just laughing about it... some guy is high in Singapore and he thinks it's the Hong Kong dollar and he keeps buying it

insights.claim: Capablanca describes HKD as a slow 'boiled frog' squeeze with a wide spread and alleged ghost locates, where shorts who entered thinking 300% up was too high were ground out rather than vertically blown up.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Distinguishes grind-up squeezes from vertical ones; ghost/uncertain locates are a warning to avoid a name entirely.
insights.quote: they get boiled like a frog... it was floating up. The spread was so wide.

insights.claim: Capablanca's first big loss was ~$60,000 shorting PETZ, a Chinese offering play where he chased the stock down toward the ~60-cent offering price before it squeezed from ~80 cents to $9 in after hours.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: The loss birthed his 'never chase offerings, short strength' rule; Chinese names don't reliably dump to dilution levels.
insights.quote: that stock went from it hits like 80 cents and then it jumped all the way to $9 in the after hours... I covered like two days later for like a 60k loss.

insights.claim: Capablanca names FFIE (Faraday Future, formerly FFAI) as his biggest winner, a luxury-EV pump-and-dump with only a prototype that ran from ~11 cents to $4-5 on multiple billion-dollar-volume days.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Example of a large, tradable pump with no real product; extreme volume plus no fundamentals is the profile he targets.

insights.claim: Capablanca points to recent ticker WK (referenced as running ~18 cents to $7 in a day, and a 'W' ticker from ~$250 to $18 to $7) as evidence that small-cap squeezes are getting more extreme and now feature unprecedented reclaims of prior highs.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Signals worsening squeeze severity and V-shaped reclaims, raising the risk of shorting even after a stock has already sold off.
insights.quote: these squeezes are getting more crazier

insights.claim: Capablanca recounts NASDAQ/NYSE cancelling all ENSC trades executed above 58 cents during a one-minute spike to ~$23 under rule 11890B, calling it a non-appealable 'trade bust' that bailed out buyers.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Traders can request a trade bust as a Hail-Mary on clearly erroneous spikes; but relief is discretionary and inconsistent.
insights.quote: pursuant to rule 1189B from NASDAQ... any trade above 58 cents executed... those trades were cancelled and they said this decision cannot be appealed.

insights.claim: Capablanca notes that similar violent spikes in other tickers (he cites PLX and another) were not trade-busted, arguing bust relief depends on whether someone connected to Wall Street complains rather than consistent rules.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Trade busts are unreliable as risk management; you cannot count on being unwound from a bad fill.

insights.claim: Capablanca characterizes Wall Street as a game of favoritism where margin-call priority, better rates, and exchange access flow to those with broker relationships, so cultivating those relationships buys tangible edge.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Relationship capital with brokers can mean extra minutes before a margin call or better locate rates, a real structural edge.
insights.quote: Wall Street is a game of favoritism... Who gets margin called first and who gets margin called last.

insights.claim: Capablanca ties the Flora Growth (FLGC) promotion to financier Stan Barti, flagged by his historical ties to a specific underwriter and photos with Jordan Belfort and Larry King as red flags of penny-stock pump-and-dumps.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Repeat-offender promoters and underwriters are a fast fundamental red flag for identifying scam small caps.

insights.claim: Capablanca notes the pending end of the Pattern Day Trader (PDT) rule and speculates it could level the playing field by letting smaller long traders compete with the larger-account short sellers who currently hold the liquidity edge.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Removing PDT could shift small-cap liquidity dynamics and the short-vs-long balance in these names.
insights.quote: now PDT is about to end

insights.claim: Capablanca navigated 2020 by treating COVID as an uncertainty/black-swan-driven mania, explicitly pattern-matching it to the dot-com boom, tulip mania, the Roaring 20s, and prior Ebola/monkeypox biotech runs.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Studying historical manias gives a template to recognize and size into a live bubble in real time.
insights.quote: 99.com boom tulip mania roaring 20s I had all these bubbles to go by... this is a mania. I need to put risk on.

insights.claim: Capablanca argues speculative behavior repeats because human emotions haven't evolved, so the stock market of 100-200 years ago behaves similarly to today and 1999 is a useful comparison for current AI-themed mania.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Positions the current AI rebrand wave as a rhyme of the .com era, supporting a skeptical, history-based read on hype names.
insights.quote: history is great because human beings, we all have the same emotions... The stock market 100 years ago, 200 years ago is pretty similar behaviors.

insights.claim: Capablanca reveals he is the founder behind Fugazi Research, disclosing it publicly for what he says is the first time, alongside Friendly Bear University and his books Short Selling Master and Trading Every Day.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Identifies the person behind a short-research outlet whose reports could move small-cap names.
insights.quote: I'm also the founder of Fugazi Research... I'm the guy behind Fugazi Research.

insights.claim: Capablanca cites Michael Marcus (from Market Wizards) taking a decade to become consistently profitable as validation of his own willingness to give trading 8-10 years before reassessing.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Reinforces a long time-horizon expectation for developing a durable trading edge, against get-rich-quick framing.
insights.quote: It took a decade for Michael Marcus.

insights.claim: Capablanca estimates he now has over 25,000 aggregated hours of study (up from ~4,000 hours at his 2020 breakout), framing edge as a compounding function of takeaways from books, podcasts, and interviewing market players.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Quantifies the study volume behind a professional small-cap short edge; skill is accumulated, not innate.
insights.quote: back then I would say I had like 4,000 hours of aggregated study time now I would say it's got to be like over 25,000 hours.

insights.claim: Capablanca argues that even after becoming consistently profitable for 6-12 months, traders must consciously break out of that plateau, noting many stay stuck at the same level for years without a breakthrough.
insights.speaker: David Capablanca
insights.so_what: Warns that early profitability can become a ceiling; scaling requires deliberately shedding a fixed self-image.
insights.quote: A lot of people stay at that same level... they're profitable, but they haven't had a breakthrough.
